state.name: Georgia
state.agency: Georgia Department of Natural Resources, Wildlife Resources Division
state.peak_intrusion_season: February through April for raccoon and squirrel denning, May through August for bat maternity colonies in attics, and a sustained year-round pressure across the southern half of the state where mild winters keep wildlife active and breeding cycles overlap
state.climate_factor: Georgia's long, humid subtropical summers and mild winters allow many nuisance species — raccoons, squirrels, opossums, rats, and armadillos — to breed multiple times per year and remain active twelve months a year, producing call volume that never fully drops off the way it does in northern states
state.permit_note: Georgia DNR requires commercial wildlife trappers to hold a Trapping License and, for properties using lethal control, a Nuisance Wildlife Control Permit; bats and migratory birds carry additional federal handling restrictions, and large game species including white-tailed deer, black bears, alligators, and feral hogs fall under direct Georgia DNR Wildlife Resources Division management rather than the private wildlife removal industry

Cobb County.region: the northwestern Atlanta metro area, immediately north of the Chattahoochee River
Cobb County.nearest_metro: Atlanta (downtown is about 20 miles southeast of Marietta)
Cobb County.habitat_type: dense Atlanta-edge suburbs with mature oak-hickory canopy, the Chattahoochee River corridor, the Kennesaw Mountain ridgeline, and pockets of older inner-ring development around Marietta and Smyrna
Cobb County.regional_wildlife: the wooded older neighborhoods of Marietta, East Cobb, and Vinings see heavy raccoon and gray squirrel pressure on attics and chimneys with mature tree canopy giving direct roof access, big brown bat colonies routinely form in the older brick-and-frame housing stock around the Marietta Square historic district and the inner Smyrna neighborhoods, armadillos have become an established and rapidly growing residential nuisance across the county — rooting up lawns and damaging foundation plantings particularly in the wooded subdivisions along the Chattahoochee corridor and Kennesaw Mountain area — and Norway rats are persistent in the older commercial corridors of Marietta, Smyrna, and the Cumberland/Vinings business district
Cobb County.absent_or_rare: white-tailed deer are abundant throughout Cobb's wooded subdivisions, black bears occasionally pass through the Kennesaw Mountain area, and the occasional alligator turns up in the Chattahoochee River corridor — but all three species fall under Georgia DNR Wildlife Resources Division management rather than the private wildlife removal contractor industry

Fulton County.region: the central metropolitan Atlanta area, the most populous county in Georgia, running from the Chattahoochee River along the western boundary through the city of Atlanta down to the southern suburbs
Fulton County.nearest_metro: Atlanta is the metro itself — Fulton contains the city's downtown core, Buckhead, Midtown, and the major north-Fulton tech and commercial corridors
natural_features: Chattahoochee River National Recreation Area (Cochran Shoals, Powers Island, Vickery Creek, Jones Bridge, Island Ford units)
Big Creek and Big Creek Greenway
Fulton County.habitat_type: metro Atlanta's most diverse housing-and-habitat mix — pre-1940 historic intown neighborhoods (Buckhead, Midtown, Inman Park, Cabbagetown, West End, Old Fourth Ward), mid-century post-war suburbs in East Point and southern Atlanta, mid-century Sandy Springs and Roswell ranch-housing zones, the Roswell historic mill village, and 1990s-2010s tech-corridor subdivisions across Alpharetta, Johns Creek, Milton, and Peachtree Corners-adjacent north Fulton
Fulton County.regional_wildlife: Fulton has the highest urban Norway rat density in metro Atlanta — the BeltLine corridor, Buckhead Village, Midtown, the Old Fourth Ward (Ponce City Market and Krog Street Market), Centennial Olympic Park downtown, and the West End commercial blocks all sustain year-round Norway rat populations in restaurant dumpster ecology and aging municipal sewer infrastructure. Big brown bat colonies in pre-1940 Atlanta intown chimneys (Buckhead older estates, West End historic district, Cabbagetown's Fulton-side row housing, Old Fourth Ward, the streets around the State Capitol) routinely span 30-60+ years of continuous occupation — among the longest-established residential bat colonies in the metro. Roof rats moved up the I-75 / GA-400 corridors during the 2000s-2010s and now drive most call volume in suburban Sandy Springs, Roswell, Alpharetta, Johns Creek, and Milton. Raccoon densities are heaviest along the Chattahoochee corridor, with year-round protein subsidy from shoreline foraging producing 15-25+ lb adults across the western edge of the county
Fulton County.absent_or_rare: white-tailed deer are largely absent from Atlanta intown but reach high densities in Milton's large-lot estate properties and along the wooded Chattahoochee corridor in north Fulton, urban coyotes are firmly established across Atlanta intown (Buckhead, West End, Old Fourth Ward) and increasingly common in north Fulton subdivisions, and federal listings include the tricolored bat (proposed for ESA listing) appearing along the Chattahoochee corridor and the bald eagle protected under the Bald and Golden Eagle Protection Act

Cherokee County.region: the northern metropolitan Atlanta exurban arc, in the rolling Piedmont uplands transitioning toward the Blue Ridge foothills
Cherokee County.nearest_metro: Atlanta (downtown is about 35-45 miles south of the Cherokee County center)
natural_features: Lake Allatoona (southern boundary, shared with Bartow and Cobb)
Cherokee County.habitat_type: Piedmont upland forest transitioning to Blue Ridge foothills, with the Etowah River corridor cutting east-to-west through the southern half of the county, Lake Allatoona shoreline forest along the southwestern boundary, Sharp Mountain and Blackjack Mountain elevation-edge habitat, Reinhardt University's wooded Waleska campus, and rapid 1990s-2010s subdivision growth across Woodstock and Holly Springs
Cherokee County.regional_wildlife: Cherokee's wide construction range — from pre-1900 mill housing in the Canton historic district through 1960s-1970s small-town infill in Ball Ground and Waleska to the massive 1990s-2010s subdivision growth across Woodstock and Holly Springs — produces a wide range of residential wildlife pressure profiles. The Etowah River corridor sustains federally listed darter populations (Cherokee darter, Etowah darter) and a continuous year-round raccoon source population. Lake Allatoona's wooded shoreline forest contributes to the bat-source population along the southern boundary. Roof rats moved up the I-575 corridor over the past two decades and now drive most suburban call volume in Woodstock and the Hwy 92 corridor
Cherokee County.absent_or_rare: white-tailed deer reach high densities in the wooded subdivisions backing up to Sharp Mountain and along the Etowah corridor, black bears occasionally appear in the northern parts of the county near the Pickens border, and the federally listed Cherokee darter and Etowah darter remain protected under the Endangered Species Act in their respective river systems

Bibb County.region: central Georgia at the geological Fall Line where the Piedmont meets the Coastal Plain, with the Ocmulgee River bisecting the county and I-75 cutting north-south through the Macon urban core, about 85 miles south of Atlanta
Bibb County.nearest_metro: Atlanta is the nearest major metro at about 85 miles north via I-75; Macon-Bibb is its own regional anchor for central Georgia
natural_features: Ocmulgee Mounds National Historical Park (federal protected pre-Columbian mound complex)
Tobesofkee Creek and Walnut Creek tributary corridors
Pleasant Hill Historic District (one of the country's oldest African-American neighborhoods)
Mercer University campus and Tattnall Square
Middle Georgia State University Macon campus
Bibb County.habitat_type: central Georgia urban-and-suburban landscape sitting on the Fall Line, with the Ocmulgee River corridor producing the dominant wildlife travel route, an exceptionally dense pre-1860 antebellum housing inventory across Macon's Vineville, In-Town, and Beall's Hill historic districts, the pre-1900 Pleasant Hill Historic District (one of the country's oldest African-American neighborhoods), the Mercer University, Wesleyan College, and Middle Georgia State University student-housing districts, mid-century post-WWII residential housing in Shirley Hills and Ingleside, and 1980s-2010s subdivision growth on the northern and western edges of Macon-Bibb. Macon-Bibb has operated as a consolidated city-county government since 2014, the largest such consolidated government in Georgia by population
common_wildlife: snakes (rat snakes, copperheads, occasional cottonmouth along Ocmulgee)
Bibb County.regional_wildlife: Bibb County's Fall Line position produces a transitional wildlife profile that mixes Piedmont species common across metro Atlanta with Coastal Plain species rarely seen north of the line. Fox squirrels (Sciurus niger) reach densities here unusual for Georgia counties further north and routinely appear in residential calls alongside the Eastern gray squirrels that dominate metro Atlanta. Brazilian free-tailed bats (Tadarida brasiliensis) form colonies in central Georgia commercial buildings and bridges that are rare in north Georgia, and they sometimes appear in Macon residential roosts in addition to the more common big brown and evening bats. Pre-1860 Vineville, In-Town, and Beall's Hill historic-district housing hosts long-established big-brown-bat colonies in original masonry chimneys — many spanning 50-100+ years of continuous occupation, putting Macon's antebellum-housing colony establishment ahead of any north Georgia metro county. Roof rats arrived in Bibb decades earlier than in metro Atlanta because central Georgia warmth supports year-round breeding cycles that sustain population growth. Norway rats are concentrated in the Mulberry Street and Cherry Street historic-downtown commercial corridors. The Ocmulgee River corridor and Ocmulgee Mounds National Historical Park sustain a continuous source population for raccoons, opossums, and Virginia rat snakes that disperses west and south into adjacent residential housing. Lake Tobesofkee shoreline forest and the Tobesofkee Creek tributary system reinforce wildlife travel habitat across western Bibb. Armadillos are firmly established countywide and have been for considerably longer than in metro-fringe counties further north — Macon-area armadillo establishment dates to at least the 1980s. Coyotes are routine across the county, particularly in the wooded subdivision edges of Shirley Hills and Ingleside. The 350,000-plus Yoshino cherry trees planted across Macon for the annual Cherry Blossom Festival create a distinctive late-March feeding ecology that produces a measurable spike in squirrel-and-bird residential pressure during bloom
Bibb County.absent_or_rare: white-tailed deer reach high densities throughout the wooded subdivision edges and along the Ocmulgee corridor, the federally proposed-for-listing tricolored bat (Perimyotis subflavus) is documented in central Georgia and may appear in pre-1860 antebellum Macon chimney colonies, American alligators occur in the lower Ocmulgee River and its slack-water reaches and any encounter over 4 feet should be referred to Georgia DNR rather than handled privately, and federally protected migratory bird species (hawks, owls, woodpeckers, chimney swifts) are present throughout the county under Migratory Bird Treaty Act protocols

Bartow County.region: the northwestern metropolitan Atlanta exurban arc, with Lake Allatoona's main basin along the eastern boundary and the Etowah River cutting through the county center
Bartow County.nearest_metro: Atlanta (downtown is about 45 miles southeast of Cartersville)
natural_features: Etowah Indian Mounds State Historic Site
Bartow County.habitat_type: rural-suburban transition zone with Lake Allatoona's main basin and Red Top Mountain State Park along the eastern boundary, the Etowah River and Etowah Indian Mounds State Historic Site near the county seat, the Pine Mountain monadnock, and a residential housing stock dominated by pre-1940 Cartersville mill housing, the 1840s-era Adairsville historic downtown, mid-century rural-suburban housing, and 1990s-2010s subdivision growth in southern Bartow toward the Cobb boundary
Bartow County.regional_wildlife: Bartow has the largest Lake Allatoona shoreline footprint of any county and the wooded shoreline forest sustains one of the densest year-round raccoon source populations in north Georgia. Pre-1940 Cartersville mill housing and the 1840s-era Adairsville historic downtown around the Train Depot host long-established big-brown-bat colonies — many spanning 30-50+ years. Roof rats moved up the I-75 corridor during the 2010s and are now establishing throughout southern Bartow; Norway rats remain dominant in the older Cartersville and Adairsville commercial corridors. The Etowah River corridor and Pumpkinvine Creek tributary system sustain continuous year-round wildlife travel habitat
Bartow County.absent_or_rare: white-tailed deer are abundant throughout Bartow's rural and rural-suburban edges, black bears occasionally pass through the more remote northern parts of the county, the occasional alligator turns up along the Etowah River corridor, and federally listed Cherokee darter and Etowah darter populations occur in the Etowah and Allatoona Creek systems

Carroll County.region: the far western metropolitan Atlanta exurban arc, on the Alabama border west of the Chattahoochee River, with I-20 cutting through the northern portion of the county
Carroll County.nearest_metro: Atlanta (downtown is about 50 miles east of Carrollton via I-20)
Carroll County.habitat_type: rural-to-exurban West Georgia Piedmont with substantial pre-1860 antebellum and Victorian historic housing in Carrollton's Maple Street Historic District, the 1850s-era Bowdon historic core, the pre-1900 Whitesburg cotton-mill town housing, the University of West Georgia campus mature canopy, the Chattahoochee River corridor along the eastern boundary, the Little Tallapoosa River cutting through the county center, the Tallapoosa River basin draining western Carroll into Alabama, and a residential housing stock that runs from antebellum Carrollton historic-district homes through mid-century rural-suburban housing to 1990s-2010s subdivision growth along the I-20 corridor between Villa Rica and Temple
Carroll County.regional_wildlife: Carroll County is the established source for armadillo dispersal across north Georgia — the species has been firmly established here for decades and continues to expand eastward into Douglas, northward into Paulding, and northwestward into Haralson and Polk. Pre-1860 Maple Street Historic District housing in Carrollton hosts long-established big-brown-bat colonies in original masonry chimneys, with many spanning 30-50+ years of continuous occupation; the 1850s Bowdon historic-core housing and the pre-1900 Whitesburg cotton-mill town housing host similar colonies. Roof rats are establishing along the I-20 corridor between Villa Rica and Temple, with Norway rats remaining dominant in the older Carrollton, Bowdon, and Whitesburg historic-downtown commercial blocks. The Chattahoochee River corridor along the eastern boundary sustains a year-round raccoon source population that disperses west into adjacent Whitesburg, Banning, and rural east-Carroll properties. The Tallapoosa River basin in western Carroll sustains substantial beaver populations that produce regular dam-and-flooding calls at rural roads, agricultural drainage, and lakefront properties throughout the western half of the county. Coyotes are firmly established countywide and routinely show up at rural homesteads, agricultural outbuildings, and the wooded subdivision edges. The University of West Georgia campus and the surrounding student-housing district produce a distinctive year-round bat and squirrel exclusion call profile centered on aging dormitory and rental-housing stock
Carroll County.absent_or_rare: white-tailed deer reach high densities throughout Carroll's rural and rural-edge areas (Carroll has one of the largest deer populations in metro-fringe Georgia), the occasional black bear passes through the western parts of the county dispersing from Alabama's Talladega National Forest source population, feral hogs occur in the more remote western and southern rural portions of the county, and federally protected migratory bird species (hawks, owls, woodpeckers) are present throughout the county under Migratory Bird Treaty Act protocols

Clarke County.region: the northeastern Georgia Piedmont, about 70 miles east of Atlanta along the I-85 corridor, anchored by the University of Georgia in Athens — the state's flagship research university and the dominant economic, cultural, and demographic driver of the county
Clarke County.nearest_metro: Atlanta (downtown is about 70 miles west of Athens via GA-316 / I-85)
natural_features: Middle Oconee River (the two rivers merge to form the Oconee just south of Athens)
Sandy Creek and Sandy Creek Park
Athens Historic District (Cobbham, Boulevard, Bloomfield, Five Points)
Memorial Park / Bear Hollow Wildlife Trail
Clarke County.habitat_type: dense urban Athens core with substantial pre-1860 antebellum and Victorian historic housing in Cobbham, Boulevard, Bloomfield, and along Milledge Avenue, plus the UGA campus mature canopy, pre-1900 mill villages in East Athens and Whitehall, mid-century Five Points and Normaltown neighborhoods, and 1990s-2010s subdivision growth on the eastern and northern edges of the county. Athens-Clarke County operates as a unified city-county government (since 1991), and the county is one of the smallest by area in Georgia at roughly 124 square miles
Clarke County.regional_wildlife: Clarke combines Athens's exceptional density of pre-1860 antebellum and Victorian historic housing (concentrated in Cobbham, Boulevard, Bloomfield, Five Points, and along Milledge Avenue) with the UGA campus's continuous mature oak-hickory canopy and the mill-village historic stock of East Athens and Whitehall. Pre-1860 antebellum Athens chimney stock hosts long-established big-brown-bat (Eptesicus fuscus) maternity colonies — many of these colonies are documented to span 50-100+ years of continuous occupation, among the longest-established residential bat colonies in north Georgia. Norway rats dominate the downtown Athens commercial corridor and UGA campus-adjacent food-service areas; roof rats are establishing in 1990s-2010s subdivisions along the eastern and northern Clarke edges. The North Oconee River and Middle Oconee River corridors and the Sandy Creek tributary system sustain continuous year-round wildlife travel habitat, with the State Botanical Garden of Georgia and Sandy Creek Park functioning as preserved-canopy source habitat
Clarke County.absent_or_rare: white-tailed deer reach high densities at the edges of Sandy Creek Park and along the North Oconee corridor, coyotes are documented across undeveloped Clarke acreage and along the Madison and Oglethorpe county boundaries, and federally protected migratory bird species (hawks, owls, woodpeckers) are present throughout the county under Migratory Bird Treaty Act protocols

Coweta County.region: the southwestern metropolitan Atlanta exurban arc, with the Chattahoochee River along the western boundary and I-85 cutting through the county center
Coweta County.nearest_metro: Atlanta (downtown is about 40 miles northeast of Newnan via I-85)
Coweta County.habitat_type: rural-suburban transition zone with Newnan's antebellum and Victorian historic housing core (the 'City of Homes'), Senoia's pre-1900 historic district (used as a film location for The Walking Dead), the Chattahoochee corridor along the western boundary, and the I-85 corridor running through the county center with substantial 1990s-2010s subdivision growth between Newnan and the Cobb/Fulton boundary
Coweta County.regional_wildlife: Coweta combines Newnan's exceptional density of antebellum and Victorian historic housing with the more rural suburban-transition character of southern Coweta. The pre-1900 Newnan historic-downtown housing — Newnan is locally known as the 'City of Homes' for its concentration of pre-Civil War and Victorian residences — hosts long-established big-brown-bat colonies in original masonry chimneys, with many spanning 30-50+ years of continuous occupation. Pre-1900 Senoia historic-district brick storefronts and surrounding housing host similar colonies. Roof rats moved up the I-85 corridor during the 2010s and now drive most call volume in southern Coweta subdivisions; Norway rats remain dominant in Newnan and Senoia historic-downtown commercial blocks. The Chattahoochee River corridor sustains a year-round raccoon source population that disperses into adjacent western Coweta subdivisions
Coweta County.absent_or_rare: white-tailed deer reach high densities in the wooded rural-edge areas of southern Coweta and along the Chattahoochee corridor, the occasional black bear passes through the more remote western parts of the county, and federally protected migratory bird species (hawks, owls, woodpeckers) are present throughout the county under Migratory Bird Treaty Act protocols

Fayette County.region: the southern metropolitan Atlanta suburban arc, anchored by the planned community of Peachtree City and the historic county seat of Fayetteville
Fayette County.nearest_metro: Atlanta (downtown is about 30 miles north of Fayetteville)
natural_features: Peachtree City's 100+ mile golf-cart path network
Fayette County.habitat_type: Atlanta's most distinctive planned-community geography — Peachtree City's master-planned residential pattern with multiple lakes (Lake Peachtree, Lake Kedron, Lake McIntosh), 100+ miles of golf-cart paths cutting through preserved greenspace, and homes that connect to lake corridors via the cart-path system; combined with Fayetteville's older historic-downtown core and Tyrone's smaller mid-century housing stock
Fayette County.regional_wildlife: Fayette's wildlife profile is shaped by Peachtree City's distinctive planned-community design. The three reservoirs (Lake Peachtree, Lake Kedron, Lake McIntosh) sustain dense year-round raccoon and bat populations along the lakeshore forest, and the 100+ mile golf-cart path network through preserved greenspace effectively functions as a continuous wildlife travel corridor connecting source habitat to residential properties. Pre-1900 Fayetteville historic-downtown housing hosts long-established big-brown-bat colonies; roof rats are dominant in Peachtree City's now-mature 1970s-1990s subdivision construction. Tyrone and the smaller north-Fayette communities have a mid-century rural-suburban housing profile
Fayette County.absent_or_rare: white-tailed deer reach exceptional densities throughout Peachtree City because of the preserved greenspace and limited deer hunting in the master-planned community, urban coyotes are now firmly established in the Peachtree City greenspace network, and federally protected migratory bird species are present throughout the county

Gwinnett County.region: the northeastern metropolitan Atlanta arc, the second-most populous county in Georgia, running from Lake Lanier on the northern boundary down through the major suburban cities to the southern Snellville and Norcross edges
Gwinnett County.nearest_metro: Atlanta (downtown is about 25-35 miles southwest of central Gwinnett)
natural_features: Sweetwater Creek (north Gwinnett, distinct from south Cobb)
Gwinnett County.habitat_type: metro Atlanta's eastern suburban-to-exurban arc, with Lake Lanier shoreline forest along the northern boundary, the Chattahoochee River corridor along the west, the Yellow River cutting through the county center, the Buford Highway commercial corridor running through the southwest, and a residential housing stock dominated by 1980s-2010s subdivision growth across Peachtree Corners, Sugar Hill, Suwanee, Duluth, and Snellville, plus the older pre-1900 Lawrenceville historic-square and 1870-established Norcross historic-downtown areas
Gwinnett County.regional_wildlife: Gwinnett has the metro's most ethnically diverse food ecosystem along the Buford Highway corridor — Korean, Vietnamese, Mexican, Chinese, Indian, Ethiopian, and Latin American restaurant rows that sustain year-round Norway rat populations in dumpster ecology. Pre-1900 Lawrenceville historic-square and 1870-established Norcross historic-downtown housing host long-established big-brown-bat colonies (30-50+ years). Roof rats moved up the I-85 corridor during the 2000s-2010s and now drive most call volume in suburban Sugar Hill, Suwanee, Peachtree Corners, Dacula, and Duluth subdivisions. Lake Lanier's shoreline forest sustains a substantial regional source population that disperses south into Sugar Hill, Suwanee, and Buford during the September-November fall window
Gwinnett County.absent_or_rare: white-tailed deer are abundant in the wooded subdivision edges throughout Gwinnett, urban coyotes are increasingly common in the older inner-Norcross and Lawrenceville blocks, the occasional alligator appears along the Yellow River corridor, and the federally proposed-for-listing tricolored bat appears along the Lake Lanier shoreline and the Chattahoochee corridor

DeKalb County.region: the eastern Atlanta metro area, sharing the city of Atlanta with Fulton County and running from Brookhaven and Dunwoody on the northern Perimeter down through Decatur and Druid Hills to Stonecrest and Lithonia on the southeastern edge
DeKalb County.nearest_metro: Atlanta (DeKalb shares the eastern half of the city of Atlanta with Fulton County)
DeKalb County.habitat_type: dense intown Atlanta neighborhoods with century-old Olmsted-designed canopy in Druid Hills and Decatur, established 1960s-1980s suburbs ringing Stone Mountain, newer subdivisions adjacent to the Arabia Mountain granite outcrops, and the South Fork Peachtree Creek and South River corridors that bisect the county
DeKalb County.regional_wildlife: DeKalb has metro Atlanta's densest concentration of pre-WWII intown housing — Decatur, Druid Hills, Avondale Estates, Oakhurst, Candler Park, Kirkwood, Lake Claire, and East Atlanta — and that 1900s-1940s housing stock sustains long-established big-brown-bat maternity colonies, persistent flying squirrel colonies in the older attic spaces, and exceptional roof rat pressure feeding off the Decatur Square, Avondale Estates, East Atlanta Village, and Brookhaven Village restaurant districts. The Olmsted-designed canopy through Druid Hills and the mature oak-hickory cover across Decatur produce some of the metro's highest gray squirrel densities. Stone Mountain Park functions as a major regional wildlife source population, with raccoons, gray squirrels, and bats dispersing into surrounding Tucker and Stone Mountain neighborhoods
DeKalb County.absent_or_rare: white-tailed deer are abundant in Stone Mountain Park and the wooded edges of Tucker and Stonecrest, urban coyotes are firmly established in Druid Hills, Decatur, and along the South Fork Peachtree Creek corridor, the federally proposed-for-listing tricolored bat is documented in the Stone Mountain and Arabia Mountain area, and the occasional black bear passes through the Stone Mountain ridgeline

Douglas County.region: the western metropolitan Atlanta suburban arc, about 25 miles west of downtown Atlanta along the I-20 corridor, with the Chattahoochee River along its eastern boundary and Sweetwater Creek cutting through the county
Douglas County.nearest_metro: Atlanta (downtown is about 25 miles east of Douglasville via I-20)
natural_features: Sweetwater Creek and Sweetwater Creek State Park
Dog River and Dog River Reservoir
New Manchester Mill ruins (within Sweetwater Creek State Park)
Douglas County.habitat_type: Piedmont upland transitioning toward the western Georgia foothills, with the Sweetwater Creek corridor and Sweetwater Creek State Park anchoring a major wildlife travel route, the Chattahoochee River along the eastern boundary, the Dog River reservoir watershed, and a residential housing stock dominated by 1980s-2010s suburban subdivisions along the I-20 corridor with a smaller pre-WWII inventory in Douglasville's historic downtown
Douglas County.regional_wildlife: Douglas County's wildlife pressure runs through the Sweetwater Creek and Chattahoochee corridors more than through any historic-housing concentration. Sweetwater Creek State Park and the surrounding wooded watershed sustain a continuous year-round source population of raccoons, opossums, and bats that disperses outward into adjacent Mirror Lake, Tributary, and New Manchester-area subdivisions. Roof rats moved into 2000s-era I-20 corridor subdivisions during the 2010s — a younger Norway rat establishment than Fulton because Douglas has far less commercial-corridor density. Eastern gray squirrels drive constant call volume from suburban canopy across Chapel Hill, Mirror Lake, Tributary, and Stewart Mill Estates. Coyotes are firmly established along the Sweetwater and Chattahoochee corridors. Armadillos are encroaching from the Carroll County direction and now turn up routinely in western Douglas yards
Douglas County.absent_or_rare: white-tailed deer are abundant along the Sweetwater Creek corridor and Chattahoochee edge, coyotes are routine across the county and increasingly bold in suburban subdivisions, the federally proposed tricolored bat appears along the Chattahoochee corridor, and federally protected migratory bird species (hawks, owls, woodpeckers) are present throughout the county under Migratory Bird Treaty Act protocols

Chatham County.region: the Atlantic coastal plain at the mouth of the Savannah River, anchored by the historic city of Savannah and bordered on the east by the Atlantic Ocean
Chatham County.nearest_metro: Savannah is the regional metropolitan center; Atlanta is roughly 250 miles inland to the northwest
natural_features: Coastal salt marsh and tidal creek system
Chatham County.habitat_type: Atlantic coastal plain with extensive salt marsh, maritime live-oak forest under Spanish moss, tidal creek and saltwater estuary systems, the Savannah and Ogeechee River corridors, the barrier islands of Tybee, Wassaw, and Skidaway, and the historic 1700s-1800s housing stock of the Savannah Historic District
common_wildlife: snakes (Eastern diamondback rattlesnake, cottonmouth, copperhead, rat snakes)
Chatham County.regional_wildlife: Chatham's wildlife profile is unlike any inland Georgia county because of the coastal geography. American alligators are present in any standing water across the county — golf course ponds, retention basins, drainage ditches, tidal creek banks, and even residential pools after storm flooding. Roof rat pressure in the Savannah Historic District is among the highest in the Southeast, sustained by tourism-district food density and 1700s-1800s housing stock with extensive structural entry-point inventory. Big brown bat and Brazilian free-tailed bat maternity colonies are routine in the historic downtown's brick row houses, attic cupolas, and church steeples. Cottonmouths (water moccasins) drive a distinctive snake call profile in waterfront and tidal-edge properties — a regional risk that Atlanta-metro counties don't share. Fox squirrels, larger and more habitat-specialized than gray squirrels, persist on the barrier-island and longleaf-pine fragments
Chatham County.absent_or_rare: American alligators are abundant and protected — encounters of any animal over 4 feet should be referred to Georgia DNR rather than handled privately, white-tailed deer are common in the suburban edges and barrier islands, sea turtles nest on Tybee and Wassaw under federal Endangered Species Act protection, the Eastern diamondback rattlesnake (the largest venomous snake in North America) is present at lower density and is treated as a serious encounter, and feral hogs occur in the western rural portions of the county
